title: Vukan R. Vuchic
authorInfo:
Emer. UPS Prof. of Transportation Engineering
Emer. Prof. of City & Regional Planning
University of Pennsylvania
Philadelphia, PA, 19104 USA
textSource:
ACADEMICIAN YURI PETROVICH BOCHAROV
In Memoriam
text:
Whev Yuri Petrovich Bocharov passed away after his 97th birthday, he left behind a very busy professional life, published works, plans and projects for city planning, lectures in dozens of countries, as well as cooperation with us, his junior colleagues.
As a boy during World War II, I became interested in Rossija. I learned Russian, watched Russian films, read Gogolj, Tolstoi and Solzhenicin, listened to Tchaikovsky, Borodin and Rachmaninov and I was interested in visiting that large country, its nature and friendly people.
Let me describe how I met Yuri Bocharov some 32 years ago and cooperated with him since that time.
I noticed an announcement in Philadelphia in 1992 that Akademik Yuri Bocharov from Moscow will present a lecture about city planning in Russia. I attended that lecture and afterward walked up to him and introduced myself. Our meeting was very lively because we noticed that we have many joint interests in city planning, urban transportation plans in different countries and, particularly, the importance of transit systems in reducing traffic congestion [probki].
I invited Yuri to be our guest at home and meet my wife Rada and our children. Academician Bocharov, being a top-level architect and painter, was very impressed by our house. He said that he had seen many beautiful houses in the United States, but this one was the best! He organized himself, collected painting materials and made several excellent water-colour paintings of our house in the Philadelphia suburbs, another of our summer home on the Atlantic Ocean, as well as the best portrait I have of myself. We still have these paintings displayed in our present apartment.
Our contacts were renewed about 2000, when I sent Yuri Bocharov my new book “Transportation for Livable Cities” and invited him to visit us again. He visited us and gave several lectures at my University of Pennsylvania in Philadelphia. In 2005, my wife Rada and I made a trip to Moscow, then traveled by boat to St. Petersburg. During our four-day stays in each city Yuri arranged for me to visit and make presentations in several transportation organizations, including the Rossiska Akademija for Architecture and Stroiteljnih Nauk - RAASN. I was warned that my audience will be mostly architects, not transportastion engineers. However, the audience showed great interest in my discusion of planning transportation to achieve livable cities, not only highways and parking garages.
Bocharov then followed through, with support by RAASN President Aleksandar Petrovich Kudrjavtzev, to get me elected as a foreign member of the - RAASN – a rare honor for me! Thus, I thank Yuri Bocharov and Aleksandar Kudrjavzev that they fulfilled my long desire to visit their great country – Rossija, and take considerable part in its transportation planning to achieve livable cities.
Our cooperation intensified when Yuri Bocharov organized with Prof. Mikhail Blinkin and Mr. A. L. Pogorel’skij to translate in Russian my book “Transport v gorodax, udobnyh dlja zhizni.” I was invited to the presentation of that translated book in 2010 and after that I was invited to several conferences about transportation improvements in Moscow, St. Petersburg, Kazan, Omsk and Ekaterinburg. My studies included solving transportation problems in Russian cities, such as highway and street designs, parking controls, tramway, trolleybus and bus systems, metro lines and pedestrian areas, an important element for livable cities.
I was also invited in 2014 by Moscow Mayor Sergey Sobyanin to serve on an international advisory committee to advise him about traffic and transportation. I assisted Yuri to reject the plan to widen Leninski Prospekt in Moscow, because it would create a high-speed highway difficult to cross between residential and office buildings toward Gagarin Square on the north.
I was invited again in 2018 also to Moscow to review the transportation conditions and progress in reducing congestion, and I submitted a brief report to the Moscow’s Deputy Mayor for Transportation Maxim Liksutov. Thus, Yuri opened up Russia and was key for me to participate in transportation planning at the highest levels.
Yuri Bocharov is one of the most competent experts I have met in several broad areas: city planning, architecture, urban design, urban transportation. He has been professor and doctoral adviser to many candidates and he has often published detailed, positive or critical reviews of buildings and entire cities, such as Tolyatti.
As this brief review of our profesional cooperation which developed into family friendship now 32 years old shows, our relationship has been mutually very fruitful and emotionally fulfilling.
Academician Yuri Petrovich Bocharov will be missed, but his publications and plans will be used by many of us, his colleagues and students, as well as city mayors and political leaders.
